title: Jetpack Components
objectives: Understand Jetpack via a real-life analogy
Know the four Jetpack pillars: Foundation, Architecture, Behavior, UI
Use Navigation, Paging, Room, WorkManager confidently

sections.markdown:
**Think of a big-box hardware store (Home Depot, Bunnings) for Android developers.**

- Before Jetpack (2018), Android was a workshop where you built your own everything: your own state-holder that survived rotation, your own hand-rolled SQLite wrapper, your own back-stack manager. Every team's kitchen was a bit different. Bugs were rampant.
- **Jetpack is the hardware store.** Google went to every serious Android team and said 'what's the pre-built solution that most of you keep re-inventing?' Then they put those solutions on the shelves: **Room** (SQLite done right), **ViewModel** (rotation-proof state), **LiveData / StateFlow** (observable state), **Navigation** (nav graphs + back-stack), **Paging** (endless lists), **WorkManager** (guaranteed background work), **Hilt** (dependency injection), **Compose** (modern UI).
- Everything comes with an aisle number (`androidx.<name>`), quality control (Google-maintained, tested), and works with the rest of the store (they compose).

**Jetpack replaced the messy 2010s Android era with a clean, documented, opinionated toolkit.** Modern Android job interviews assume you use Jetpack — asking 'how do you survive rotation?' expects 'ViewModel' as the first word.

**Android Jetpack** is a suite of Google-maintained libraries (`androidx.*`), tools, and guidance that helps you follow best practices. Launched at Google I/O 2018, it consolidated the fragmented Android Support Library era into one coherent umbrella with **four categories**: Foundation, Architecture, Behavior, and UI.
## The four Jetpack pillars

rows: **ViewModel, LiveData, Lifecycle**, Room, Navigation, Paging, WorkManager, DataStore, Hilt

sections.markdown: **The most-interviewed Jetpack combination:** ViewModel + StateFlow (or LiveData) + Repository + Room + Coroutines. If you can explain that stack cleanly — how data flows from Room through a repository to a ViewModel and out to the UI as observable state — you have the modern Android answer to about 40% of Android interview questions.
## The Architecture components deep-dive

rows: Survives config changes; scoped to Activity/Fragment/NavGraph
Only emits to STARTED/RESUMED owners; retired in favor of StateFlow in new code
Cold/hot flows, backpressure-aware, works with `collectAsState()` in Compose
Underpins the whole architecture — every -aware component observes it
Type-safe args, back-stack, deep-linking, multi-tab back-stacks
Compile-time SQL validation; Flow/LiveData return types

sections.markdown: ## The canonical Jetpack sample — ViewModel + StateFlow + Repository
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Instantiating a ViewModel with `ViewModel()`.** Direct construction creates a new instance every rotation — losing all state. Fix: use `by viewModels()` (Fragment/Activity) or `by activityViewModels()` (shared). These use the correct `ViewModelProvider` internally.

**Mistake 2: Collecting a StateFlow with `launch { flow.collect { ... } }` in a Fragment.** This survives past `onDestroyView`, wasting work and possibly crashing. Fix: wrap with `repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED)` so collection pauses when the view isn't visible.

**Mistake 3: Storing a Context or View reference in a ViewModel.** ViewModel outlives the Activity; holding its Context leaks the Activity. Use `AndroidViewModel(app)` if you truly need an Application context.

**Mistake 4: Using LiveData for cold data streams.** LiveData is designed for state, not events (its 'last value' replay behavior is wrong for one-shot events). Modern replacements: `StateFlow` for state, `SharedFlow` for events, or SingleLiveEvent wrapper.

**Mistake 5: Skipping the Repository layer.** ViewModel talking directly to Retrofit and Room mixes concerns and makes testing painful. A Repository owns the merge policy (network + cache) and gives the ViewModel one clean API.
## Best practices
**1. Follow the recommended architecture:** UI → ViewModel → Repository → Data Sources (Room / Retrofit). One direction of data flow.
**2. UI state is one data class.** Emit a single `HomeUi` from ViewModel — not five separate LiveDatas. Reduces re-render churn and makes the UI a pure function of state.
**3. Use `viewModelScope` and `lifecycleScope` for coroutines.** They auto-cancel on VM/lifecycle destruction — no manual job management needed.
**4. Use `repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED)` for Flow collection in Fragments.** Pauses when hidden; resumes when visible; cleans up on destroy.
**5. Inject dependencies with Hilt.** Google's opinionated Dagger for Android; less boilerplate, lifecycle-aware scopes.
**6. Prefer StateFlow / SharedFlow to LiveData in new code.** Better coroutine integration, richer operators, works identically well in Compose.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — ViewModel counter.** ViewModel with a `count: StateFlow<Int>`. Fragment observes and displays. Rotate — count survives.

**Why:** Sanity check the whole ViewModel + StateFlow flow.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Room + Flow + ViewModel.** Room DB with User entity; DAO returns `Flow<List<User>>`; ViewModel exposes it; Fragment consumes.

**Why:** Full modern data-flow pipeline.

**Practice 3 (Medium) — Navigation graph.** Two fragments, typed args via Safe Args, back button works. Add a deep link.

**Why:** Real navigation patterns.

**Practice 4 (Hard) — Paging 3 list.** Load 10K users from an API into a paginated RecyclerView; show placeholders.

**Why:** Real production lists use Paging.

**Practice 5 (Expert) — Full architecture.** UI + ViewModel + Repository + Room + Retrofit, wired with Hilt, with unit tests for the ViewModel using a fake Repository.

**Why:** Interview reveal — this is the app you can talk about in every senior interview.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Android Jetpack?**

**A:** Jetpack is Google's opinionated suite of libraries, tools, and guidance for building modern Android apps. Launched at Google I/O 2018, it consolidated the fragmented Android Support Library era into one umbrella with four categories: **Foundation** (backwards compatibility, testing), **Architecture** (ViewModel, LiveData, Room, WorkManager, Navigation, Hilt), **Behavior** (permissions, notifications, sharing), **UI** (Compose, Fragment, animation). Libraries live under the `androidx.*` package namespace. Jetpack replaced the '2010s wild-west' era where every team hand-rolled state management, DB wrappers, and background workers, giving Android a coherent, tested, documented toolkit. Modern interviews assume Jetpack fluency.

**Q2: What is a ViewModel and what problem does it solve?**

**A:** A ViewModel is a class that holds UI state and business logic, scoped to an Activity, Fragment, or NavGraph. Its killer property: it **survives configuration changes** (rotation, dark mode, locale) that would normally destroy the Activity. So the user rotates the phone → the Activity is destroyed and recreated → the new Activity gets the SAME ViewModel instance → all state is preserved. Before ViewModel, we hacked this with `onSaveInstanceState` bundles (limited size) or `Fragment.setRetainInstance(true)` (fragile). ViewModel + coroutines + `viewModelScope` also handles cancellation cleanly — the scope is cancelled when the ViewModel is finally destroyed. To also survive process death, wrap with `SavedStateHandle`.

**Q3: LiveData vs StateFlow — which to use?**

**A:** **LiveData** (Jetpack, 2017) — lifecycle-aware observable holding a single value. Auto-pauses when the observer is not STARTED/RESUMED, no leak worries. Easy to use, main-thread only, replay-based semantics. **StateFlow** (Kotlin Coroutines, 2020) — coroutine-native observable, part of Kotlin Flow. Richer operators (`map`, `combine`, `debounce`), thread-flexible, better testability. **Recommendation for new code:** StateFlow (or SharedFlow for events). It composes cleanly with Compose (`collectAsState()`), other Flow-returning APIs (Room, DataStore), and coroutines. LiveData is fine to keep in existing codebases but Google's samples increasingly use Flow. **Key gotcha:** collect StateFlow with `repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED)` in Fragments — the equivalent of LiveData's automatic lifecycle awareness.

**Q4: What is the recommended architecture (Google's official)?**

**A:** A three-layer, one-way flow:
- **UI layer** — Activities/Fragments/Compose observe state from a ViewModel and emit user events back. Dumb; renders from state.
- **Domain layer** *(optional)* — use-cases / interactors that hold business logic when it doesn't fit cleanly in the data or UI layer.
- **Data layer** — Repositories are the single source of truth for a data type. Each Repository combines one or more DataSources (Room, Retrofit, DataStore, in-memory cache). It exposes reactive streams (Flow / LiveData / suspend fun).

**Data flows down (Repo → VM → UI), events flow up (UI → VM → Repo).** One-way. This makes state predictable, testable, and easy to reason about. It's essentially MVVM/MVI in Android form.

**Q5: What's `viewModelScope` and how does it work?**

**A:** `viewModelScope` is a `CoroutineScope` extension property on `ViewModel`. Any coroutine launched with `viewModelScope.launch { ... }` runs on `Dispatchers.Main.immediate` by default and is automatically **cancelled** when the ViewModel is cleared (i.e. when its owner is finally destroyed, not on rotation). This eliminates the classic bug: launching a coroutine that finishes AFTER the Activity dies, updating a dead view. **Pair with `lifecycleScope`** (on Activity/Fragment) for UI-observation coroutines that must stop when the UI is destroyed. Rule of thumb: business logic → `viewModelScope`; UI observation → `lifecycleScope` + `repeatOnLifecycle`.

**Q6: When would you use WorkManager vs a Service vs a Coroutine?**

**A:** **Coroutine (viewModelScope/lifecycleScope)** — short work tied to a UI: network fetch, DB read, animation. Cancels with the UI. **Service (foreground)** — user-visible ongoing work: music playback, active tracking, an in-progress upload with a notification. **WorkManager** — deferrable, guaranteed background work that must survive process death and app restarts: uploading a log file when Wi-Fi is available, nightly data sync, exporting to CSV. WorkManager handles constraints (network, charging), retries, backoff, and survives reboots. Since Android 8, background Services are heavily restricted — WorkManager is the modern replacement for most of them.

**Q7: What are Jetpack's `lifecycle-aware` components?**

**A:** Any component that observes the lifecycle of a `LifecycleOwner` (Activity/Fragment) and reacts automatically. Instead of you calling `component.onStart()` / `onStop()`, the component observes and reacts. Examples: **LiveData** — stops emitting to observers below STARTED; **`lifecycleScope`** — cancels coroutines on DESTROY; **`repeatOnLifecycle(STARTED)`** — restarts a Flow collection on each STARTED transition; **Camera / Location** components — automatically bind/unbind. **How to build one:** implement `DefaultLifecycleObserver` and register with a `Lifecycle.addObserver(this)`. This pattern eliminates a huge class of 'forgot to cleanup' bugs.

**Q8: What is the Navigation component and why use it?**

**A:** Jetpack Navigation replaces manual `FragmentTransaction` code with a **declarative nav graph** (XML or Kotlin DSL) that lists every destination and every action (edge) between them. Benefits: (1) **Safe Args** — typed argument classes generated from the graph, no more string-keyed extras. (2) **Automatic back-stack** management including `popUpTo` and inclusive-pop semantics. (3) **Deep linking** declared in the graph with URI patterns. (4) Multi-back-stack support for bottom-nav apps. (5) Works with fragments AND Compose destinations (`androidx.navigation:navigation-compose`). Since 2021 it's the recommended way to structure any Activity + Fragments app.

## Real-world usage

- **Every modern Android production app** uses at minimum: ViewModel, Lifecycle, and a Repository pattern.
- **`androidx.compose`** — Jetpack Compose is now the recommended UI toolkit for new apps.
- **Google's Now In Android app** — the canonical reference architecture using every Jetpack library.
- **Play Store apps you've used** — Instagram, WhatsApp, Reddit, Signal all use various Jetpack libraries.
- **Enterprise / MNC codebases** — TCS/Infosys/Accenture Android projects often gradually migrated to Jetpack architecture components; being able to lead that migration is a senior-level skill.
- **Interview theme** — 'walk me through your app's architecture' — Jetpack is the vocabulary you use to answer.

## Advanced notes

- **`AndroidViewModel(application)`** — a ViewModel with a safe `Application` context.
- **`SavedStateHandle`** — inject it into a ViewModel constructor; makes state survive process death (backed by the saved-instance bundle).
- **`by viewModels { factory }`** — provide a custom `ViewModelProvider.Factory` when your ViewModel has constructor args.
- **Hilt integration** — `@HiltViewModel` + constructor injection; Hilt provides the factory automatically.
- **CompositionLocal in Compose** — Compose's equivalent of Lifecycle-aware dependency provision.
- **`SharedFlow`** for events (non-replayed by default; use `MutableSharedFlow(extraBufferCapacity=1)` for one-shot events).
- **`stateIn` operator** — turn a cold Flow into a hot StateFlow with a sharing policy (`WhileSubscribed`, `Lazily`, `Eagerly`).
- **Testing** — Jetpack ships test artifacts: `androidx.arch.core:core-testing` for LiveData/InstantTaskExecutorRule, coroutine `TestScope`, Room in-memory DB for DAO tests.

flashcards.back: Survives config changes (rotation), scoped to Activity/Fragment/NavGraph.
StateFlow is preferred in new code — coroutine-native, richer, Compose-friendly. LiveData is fine for existing code.
UI → ViewModel → Repository → DataSource. One-way data flow.

flashcards.front: How to collect Flow safely in a Fragment?

flashcards.back: Guaranteed deferrable background work with constraints (network, charging), surviving reboots.

quiz.explain: Config changes automatically; pair with SavedStateHandle for process death.

quiz.explain: Ensures collection pauses when view is not visible.
WorkManager is the modern API for it.

challenges.text: Build a counter ViewModel exposing `StateFlow<Int>`, observed by a Fragment.
Add SavedStateHandle so the counter survives process death.
Wire Room DAO returning `Flow<List<User>>` → Repository → ViewModel → UI.
Set up Navigation with 2 fragments, Safe Args typed arguments, and a deep link.
Add Paging 3 to a list backed by a Retrofit endpoint.
Build the canonical Jetpack app: UI + VM + Repo + Room + Retrofit + Hilt + tests.
